text: The surgeon begins the dissection by mobilizing the greater curvature of the stomach, using an ultrasonic or bipolar advanced energy device to take down the short gastric vessels. A safe margin of tissue is maintained away from the gastric serosa to prevent thermal injury.
The surgical team utilizes a lateral assistant port to enhance exposure and expedite the dissection process during the laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication.

The surgeon approaches the spleen, sharply dissecting posterior adhesions to the stomach to enhance its mobility and improve visualization near the splenic hilum. Short gastric vessels between the stomach and spleen are carefully taken down without using thermal energy.

The surgeon places a marking stitch at the level of the fundoplication on the fully mobilized stomach, ensuring the wrap does not extend to the body of the stomach. A 50 to 56 French bougie is inserted under direct visualization to guide the loose fundoplication.

The surgeon begins the 360-degree fundoplication by placing a grasper behind the esophagus, pulling the marking stitch through. The stomach is manipulated back and forth using the shoe shine technique to achieve a loose, floppy wrap around the esophagus.

The surgeon secures the fundoplication using three interrupted non-absorbable sutures, tying them extracorporeally for enhanced knot security. The initial suture is placed, and the marking stitch is removed.
The assistant port holds the stomach in position while the surgeon secures the fundoplication with three interrupted stitches, creating a wrap approximately three centimeters in length.

The surgeon removes the bougie and performs an intraoperative esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) to confirm the anatomical configuration. A probe is passed under the completed wrap to visually verify the loose and floppy nature of the fundoplication.
